channel.title: Beginner's Guide to Group Field Theory
channel.description: Beginner's Guide to Group Field Theory

item.title: 6: Starting the Action
item.dc:creator: Tom Whitehead
item.description:
1 Pieces of the action

In the last post we wrote the fields defined over our group in terms of representations of the group. These fields included physical requirements of closure and simplicity, generating the final form of the field as

where are the field components, are the intertwiners that implement the closure condition, and are the representation matrices of the group elements. The labels are representation labels, and the labels and are the indices of the representation matrices. The field is a function of four group elements, which we can think of as being associated with the four faces of a tetrahedron.

In a previous post we also discussed the Casimir invariants of the group and their form in a given representation. It’s now time to bring these together and use the Casimir invariants to build an action for our universe.

Casimir invariants are a natural choice as building blocks to construct a group-invariant action, because they are themselves invariant under every generator of the group. So let’s start with a simple example: taking the expectation value of the Casimir invariant under our group field in the standard group basis:

This is a perfectly good group-invariant action, but it is not very useful in this form. We can rewrite it in terms of the representation basis using the Peter-Weyl transform, which will give us a much more manageable expression. The key observation is that the Casimir invariant acts on the representation matrices as a scalar, multiplying them by the eigenvalue of the Casimir invariant in that representation. This means that when we rewrite the action in terms of the representation basis, we will get an expression that is diagonal in the representation labels , and that involves the eigenvalues of the Casimir invariant in those representations.

The corresponding action in the representation basis is

where each face carries the two representation labels , and the Casimir eigenvalue follows from the general formula for the representation . The sum over accounts for the Casimir acting independently on each of the four group arguments of the field. The are the matrix indices of the representation, summed over all states within each representation. Under , representations are labelled by a pair ; the simplicity constraint restricts to run only over the diagonal sector.

1.1 Other Casimirs

The part of the action above corresponding to the invariant is just part of the story. We can also include other Casimir invariants, such as , , etc: each of these gives us a different group-invariant action, and we can take linear combinations of them to build more complicated actions. We now run into a free parameter problem: what should the relative weights in the linear combination of these different Casimir invariants be?

This is a fundamental problem with the group field theory framework: the construction of the action is not determined by the framework itself, but requires input from physics. For now we will note that the part of the action second-order in the fields can be written as

where , , , etc are the free parameters corresponding to the different Casimir invariants, and is a weight corresponding to the trivial identity invariant. The , , , etc are the eigenvalues of the corresponding Casimir invariants in the representation .

1.2 Shape of the action

Previously we have discussed how the fields can be associated with tetrahedra, with each of the four arguments of the field corresponding to a face of the tetrahedron. In the quadratic term Equation 2 we saw that the faces of these tetrahedra were exactly paired up between the two terms: both terms have faces . This means that the quadratic part of the action corresponds to dual tetrahedra, that share all of their faces. In principle we could include terms with arbitrary numbers of fields in the action, but in order for the action to be a scalar we must ensure that all faces of the tetrahedra are paired up between the fields.

This is where we will introduce another constraint on the mathematical framework to ensure it generates a universe that looks like ours. We will ‘colour’ each of the faces of the tetrahedra a different colour, and only allow terms in the action where faces of the same colour are paired up between the fields. This effectively introduces an internal symmetry on the fields.

This has a simple interpretation for the quadratic term above: all pairs of matching colours pair up. As we introduce more fields into the action, it gets considerably more complicated. In Figure 1 we show the possible ways to pair up the faces of fields, with each small circle representing a field (a tetrahedron), and each line representing a pair of faces of the same colour. The quadratic action from above is shown in the top row, with four faces of matching colours paired up between the two fields. At cubic order in the fields there is only one way to pair up the faces, shown in the second row: each field shares two faces with each of the other two fields. Fourth and fifth order in the fields have multiple possible constructions, and this is the source of a lot of the complexity in group field theory.

In fact, there is too much complexity in the allowed ways to pair up the faces of the fields. We will examine in future posts how the group actions we’re building ends up generating a physical universe, and we will see that many of the allowed pairings in Figure 1 do not generate a universe that looks like ours. For now we will simply note that the pairings that do generate a universe that looks like ours are only the quadratic pairing shown in the top row, and the simplicial quintic pairing shown in the bottom right. This is a very strong constraint on the form of the action, the underlying physical reason for which is not yet fully understood.

2 Bringing it all together

To summarise, the action of our universe is built from pairings of the faces of the fields. These are acted on linearly by the Casimir invariants, with free parameters generating the linear combinations of the different Casimir invariants. So the action, including the quadratic and simplicial quintic terms, has the general form

where the label the representations associated with the faces of the five fields in the quintic term; the are the intertwiners associated with each of the five fields; the is a symbol that captures the combinatorial structure of the quintic term, and is a function of the representation labels and intertwiners; and the are the matrix indices of the representations, summed over all states within each representation.

This is the action of our universe, built from the symmetries of the group. This is the point of maximum complexity of the theory. From here on we will be simplifying down to recover physics: beginning next time with asking how we can recover fermionic symmetries from the (so far apparently bosonic) group field theory framework.

item.title: 4: Fields on the Group
item.dc:creator: Tom Whitehead
item.description:
1 Defining fields

So far we have discussed symmetries of our theory, and the mathematical structures that underly them. But we haven’t yet had any dynamical, physical objects that can act under those symmetries. This post will introduce the concept of fields on our group, and we will see how the symmetries of the group act on those fields and strongly constrain their form.

Recall that our full group is . A ‘group field’ is a complex-valued function defined on copies of this group, , where each is a group element. This is the central dynamical object of our theory. Let’s now dive into what this field means, and how it connects to the symmetries of our group.

2 What is a field?

At this point the field is a purely group-theoretic object: it lives on copies of the group manifold , and we have no connection to any physical theory. The question of what should be, and what geometric picture (if any) emerges, will be answered from the group structure alone. We will now impose two natural constraints on the field, gauge invariance and simplicity, and show that together they force to take a specific value and endow the field with a geometric interpretation.

2.1 Gauge invariance and closure

The first constraint comes from gauge symmetry. We want that the physics of our universe does not change if we ‘rotate’ our viewpoint of the fields using any of the symmetries of the group. This can be written by requiring that the field is invariant under a group element acting simultaneously on every argument of the field by right multiplication:

This is the statement that the physics does not depend on a simultaneous ‘rotation’ of all arguments by the same group element. At the level of the Lie algebra, this gauge invariance translates into a ‘closure constraint’: the Lie algebra elements (or ‘fluxes’) associated with each argument must sum to zero,

Think of this like conservation of angular momentum: the total flux across all arguments must balance. Since the algebra decomposes as a direct sum (see the first post), this closure constraint holds independently for each factor. The constraint does not yet tell us what is, but as we will see, when combined with a second constraint it will give a powerful geometric interpretation.

2.2 The maximal compact subgroup and

To go further, we need to look more carefully at the structure of . This group is non-compact (because of the in the metric signature), and its representation theory is correspondingly complicated. However, every non-compact semisimple Lie group has a ‘maximal compact subgroup’, which captures much of the representation-theoretic structure while being far easier to work with.

For , the maximal compact subgroup is : the group of rotations in four-dimensional Euclidean space, corresponding to the generators with (i.e. the spatial indices only, excluding the timelike 0 direction). It is this subgroup that the simplicity constraint will act on.

The algebra has a beautiful structure. Its generators are the six antisymmetric matrices with , which we can organise into two sets of three by defining the self-dual and anti-self-dual combinations:

where . One can verify using the commutation relations above (restricted to spatial indices with ) that these satisfy

The two sets of generators decouple completely! This means the algebra splits as a direct sum:

Therefore the representations of are labelled by a pair of spins. This is a key result: the representation theory of is fully captured by two independent spin labels.

2.3 The simplicity constraint

Each argument of the field lives in (times the internal groups). At the Lie algebra level, the data associated with each argument is an element (compare to the way a vector is defined on a basis). The generators are antisymmetric in their indices (), so is characterised by the 10 independent components . Because is defined analogously to a vector on a vector space, but is a rank-2 object, it is called a ‘bivector’.

Now, a general bivector is a sum of independent ‘wedge products’: it can encode data from multiple independent planes simultaneously. In , which has a 5-dimensional defining representation, a generic bivector can be decomposed into at most independent simple (wedge-product) pieces. The ‘simple’ bivectors are those that consist of a single wedge product: for some vectors , .

There is a clean algebraic condition for simplicity. A bivector is simple if and only if

The left-hand side is formed by contracting with the totally antisymmetric Levi-Civita symbol. This expression vanishes if and only if is simple (i.e. ). When is instead a sum of two or more independent wedge products, the contraction is non-zero. This condition is purely algebraic and it is called the ‘simplicity constraint’.

2.4 Simplicity at the level of representations

Now let’s translate this condition into representation theory, using the decomposition from Equation 5, and apply it to our field. An arbitrary element of decomposes into self-dual and anti-self-dual parts via Equation 3:

The simplicity condition Equation 6, when restricted to the sector (indices 1–4 only), becomes

where are the norms of the self-dual and anti-self-dual parts respectively.

NoteDerivation of Equation 7 from Equation 6

In four dimensions, the simplicity condition can be evaluated by expanding the Levi-Civita symbol. The result is easiest in the self-dual/anti-self-dual basis. Using the decomposition and , the simplicity condition becomes

Recall from Equation 5 that representations are labelled . The Casimir invariant of each factor is , with eigenvalue in the representation labelled by .

In the next post we will take the Peter-Weyl transform of the field, analogous to a Fourier transform on group space. To see what the simplicity constraint becomes in this picture, consider the analogy with ordinary Fourier analysis. A function on a line can be expanded in modes . Classically, momentum is a number ; but in the Fourier picture, the role of momentum is played by the differential operator , whose eigenvalues are the Fourier labels . The classical quantity is replaced by — a new discrete (or continuous) parameter that labels the modes.

The same structure applies to functions on a group. Our field is a function on the group, and the Peter-Weyl theorem expands it in irreducible representation matrix elements (the group-theoretic analogue of ). The Lie algebra flux , which classically is just a set of numerical components, plays the role of momentum: it is replaced by the generator , which acts on functions on the group by differentiation (a left-invariant vector field), and whose Casimir eigenvalue is the new label that characterises each mode. So the correspondence is: (classical flux) (representation label), just as (classical momentum) (Fourier label). The classical simplicity condition therefore becomes a condition on these new labels: , and since spins are non-negative, this gives

This is the simplicity constraint in representation-theoretic language: it restricts the representations that appear in our field to the diagonal ones, where the self-dual and anti-self-dual spins are equal.

This is a powerful result. It means that each argument of our field, when we decompose its content into representations, must carry equal left and right spins.

3 From simple bivectors to triangles

Before we can determine , we need to understand what each argument of the field represents after simplicity has been imposed.

The maximal compact subgroup of is , which acts naturally on . A simple bivector in is an element for two vectors . Geometrically, this is the oriented area element of the parallelogram spanned by and : or equivalently, of a triangle with two edges along and . So the simplicity constraint has told us, purely from the algebra, that each argument of the field carries the data of a triangular face embedded in .

4 Why

We can now combine the closure constraint (Section 2.1) with the geometric content of simplicity (Section 3). The closure constraint on the sector requires

where each is a simple bivector representing a triangular face. This algebraic condition on the bivectors is a consequence of the gauge invariance Equation 1 that we already imposed. With the interpretation of as the oriented area of a triangle, the closure condition implies that the oriented area data associated with the arguments of the field must form a closed surface; open configurations are not gauge-invariant and not allowed.

The mathematical content of this closure is captured by the ‘generalised Minkowski theorem’: a set of simple bivectors in that sum to zero correspond one-to-one with the oriented face areas of a closed convex polyhedron. In our case , as determined by .

So which closed polyhedron? The field has a fixed number of arguments, and our task is to find the minimal value of for which closure is possible. We want the minimal value of because we want the field to be as simple as possible. If we constructed a non-minimal polyhedron with more faces, we could always triangulate it into a collection of smaller polyhedra with fewer faces, and therefore build it from products of fields with fewer arguments. The minimal value of gives us the fundamental building block of geometry in our theory.

A closed surface made of triangles requires at least 4 triangular faces. The unique closed triangulated surface with exactly 4 faces is the boundary of a tetrahedron.

Notice the logical chain: we chose as the symmetry group its maximal compact subgroup is simple bivectors in describe triangles in the minimal set of triangles satisfying the closure constraint is a tetrahedron with 4 faces

Our field therefore takes the form

subject to the gauge invariance Equation 1 and the simplicity constraint Equation 8. Each such field describes the state of a single tetrahedron, equipped with internal gauge charges from the factors.

Let’s summarise the process we have carried out in this post. We did not (and will not for some time) use any spacetime manifold or dimension. We defined a field on copies of an abstract group, imposed natural constraints (gauge invariance and simplicity), and the group told us the answer: because has as its maximal compact subgroup, the geometry that emerges is four-dimensional, and the fundamental building block is a tetrahedron. Had we started with (with maximal compact ), simple bivectors in would be vectors in ; the closure constraint would give a closed polygon of edges; the simplest such polygon would be a triangle with , and we would be building three-dimensional geometry.

Later in this series we will see how the dynamics of the theory describe how these tetrahedra glue together, and how spacetime emerges from their collective behaviour. But for now, we have a beautiful geometric picture of the fundamental ‘atom of geometry’ in our theory: the group field describes a single tetrahedron, with its four arguments encoding the geometry of its four triangular faces.

In the next post we will come back to the Peter-Weyl transform mentioned above and bring our Casimir operators back to the fore, beginning to form the foundations of the dynamics of our theory.

item.title: 5: The Peter-Weyl Transform
item.dc:creator: Tom Whitehead
item.description:
1 The Peter-Weyl transform

Last time we introduced the concept of fields on our group, and we saw how the symmetries of the group act on those fields. We will now do the equivalent of ‘going to momentum space’ in our theory, by performing a Peter-Weyl transform on the field.

2 The Peter-Weyl transform and the Haar measure

Now that we know the field lives on , we would like to decompose it into modes:the analogue of a Fourier transform. On a Lie group, the correct tool is the Peter-Weyl theorem, which generalises the Fourier transform from the circle group to an arbitrary compact group.

For a compact group , the Peter-Weyl theorem states that the matrix elements of irreducible representations form a complete orthonormal basis for square-integrable functions on with respect to the Haar measure . The Haar measure is the unique (up to normalisation) left- and right-invariant measure on the group: it is the natural ‘volume element’ that respects the group’s symmetries.

For our compact factors this is straightforward. On , the Haar measure can be parametrised using the Euler angles as

with ranges , , . For , the Haar measure involves eight parameters (corresponding to the eight generators) and is considerably more involved, but has a similar essential character. For , the Haar measure is simply .

For the non-compact factor , the situation is more subtle. We need to use a generalization of the Peter-Weyl theorem known as the Plancherel decomposition which handles non-compact groups. The sum over representations becomes an integral, weighted by the Plancherel measure , which depends on the representation labels introduced in the representations post.

To write the Haar measure on explicitly, we use the Cartan decomposition , where is the maximal compact subgroup discussed in the last post and is a one-dimensional abelian subgroup parametrised by a ‘boost’ parameter . This decomposition says that any element of can be written as a rotation, followed by a boost, followed by another rotation. The Haar measure then factorises as

We can make this completely explicit using the decomposition from the self-dual/anti-self-dual splitting discussed last time. (Recall that the algebra splits as a clean direct sum ; the quotient only appears at the group level because the element acts trivially on , making the product a double cover of . For the Haar measure this is invisible: the quotient is discrete, so it only halves the total volume, which is absorbed into the normalisation.) Each Haar measure factorises into two Euler-angle measures,

with the same ranges as the measure above: , , . Writing subscripts for the two copies and expanding everything out, the full Haar measure on reads

with thirteen integration variables (): twelve Euler angles and one boost parameter . Note that is only ten-dimensional; the three extra variables reflect the redundancy of the decomposition: the centraliser (dim 3) acts by , so different triples can describe the same group element. This overcounting is absorbed into the normalisation constant.

The factor is characteristic of the de Sitter geometry: it reflects the fact that the volume of a sphere in hyperbolic space grows exponentially with radius. In the flat limit (corresponding to ), , recovering the familiar Euclidean volume element.

The full Haar measure on is the product of four copies of each factor’s measure.

2.1 The mode expansion

Why did we need the Haar measure? The Peter-Weyl theorem says that the matrix elements of the irreducible representations form a complete orthonormal basis for : the space of square-integrable functions on the group with respect to the Haar measure. The measure defines the inner product, giving meaning to the phrase ‘orthonormal basis’. In other words, the Haar measure is to what is to : the weight that makes the Fourier-type expansion work.

The Peter-Weyl (or Plancherel) expansion of our field then reads:

where collectively denotes all the representation labels for each copy of (i.e.  for , for , for , and for ), and are the corresponding matrix elements of the irreducible representations (Wigner D-matrices and their generalisations). For the factor, the sum over is replaced by an integral weighted by the Plancherel measure. The simplicity constraint restricts the content of the representations to the diagonal sector.

2.2 Gauge invariance in representation space

The gauge invariance has a striking effect when translated into this representation basis. Recall that gauge invariance requires for all . Under right multiplication by , the matrix elements transform as

Substituting the expansion Equation 1 into the gauge invariance condition and using the orthogonality of the representation matrices, we find that the coefficients must satisfy

for all . By Schur’s lemma, this is only possible if the indices are contracted with an ‘intertwiner’: a tensor that is invariant under the simultaneous action of on all four representation spaces:

Such intertwiners are built from Clebsch-Gordan coefficients: the tensors that couple pairs of representations into a third. For example, for the part of the group, one first couples into an intermediate representation , and then into the same , and contracts. The space of such intertwiners for four representations is generally multi-dimensional—labelled by the intermediate coupling —which is why the intertwiner carries additional data beyond the four representation labels.

The gauge-invariant field therefore takes the form

where the sum over runs over a basis of the intertwiner space for the given set of representations . The coefficients are the remaining dynamical degrees of freedom. In the spin foam literature, the intertwiner is often called a ‘spin network vertex’: it encodes the quantum state of the tetrahedron’s ‘shape’ (how the four face areas are coupled together), while the representation labels encode the areas of the individual faces.

There we have it: a field defined on our group, including the constraints imposed by physical requirements, written in terms of the representation data that will allow us to connect to particles and fields in our emergent universe. Next time we will see how Casimir invariants act on these fields, and begin to move towards identifying aspects of the emergent physics.

item.title: 3: The Lorentz Connection
item.dc:creator: Tom Whitehead
item.description:
1 The algebra

This post will begin the journey from pure group theory towards a connection with the universe we are creating. The name ‘Lorentz connection’ is a bit of a pun, as we will be looking at how connects to the Lorentz algebra of symmetries in special relativity, but we won’t actually be building any spin connections or other explicit geometric structures in this post.

To start with, let’s remind ourselves of the algebra . This was given in the first post as the algebra with generators , where and run from 0 to 4, and commutation relations

where . We will now connect this algebra back to the familiar maths of special relativity.

To start with, we will do a simple relabelling of the generators. Let’s define indices and that run from 0 to 3, and define and , where is a constant with dimensions of length. We can now rewrite the commutation relations in terms of these new generators:

Remember this is just a relabelling, none of the symmetries of the group have changed. But now we have some suggestive-shaped algebra: the first line is the algebra of Lorentz transformations, , and the second line says that the transform as a four-vector under Lorentz transformations. This line is also familiar from the Poincaré algebra of special relativity. The third line, however, is different from special relativity: it says that two translations do not commute, but instead give a rotation. This is a key feature of the algebra , and it will have important consequences for the physics that emerges from this algebra. We write the separation of the algebra into these two parts as , where is the part of the algebra generated by the . The group corresponding to the algebra is called the de Sitter group, and is represented as the quotient group .

We can think of the non-commuting of translations like the behaviour of translations on a sphere: if you start at the north pole and translate down to the equator, then translate along the equator, and then translate back up to the north pole, you will be facing in a different direction than you started. This is a consequence of the curvature of the sphere, and it implies that the universe we are building also has a built-in curvature.

If we take the limit , the commutation relations reduce to those of the Poincaré algebra, which is the algebra of symmetries of flat spacetime in special relativity. This means that the algebra can be thought of as a ‘deformation’ of the Poincaré algebra, where the deformation parameter is .

2 Casimir invariants in our new basis

The Casimir invariants of can also be rewritten in terms of the new generators. Recall that there is a second order and a fourth order Casimir invariant. Replacing the , indices with , and the 4 index with gives us

where is the Casimir invariant of the Lorentz algebra , and . The fourth order Casimir invariant is given by

where is the dual of the second order Casimir invariant of the Lorentz algebra, and is the Pauli-Lubanski vector from special relativity. So although our algebra is definitely 5-dimensional, it contains the familiar structures of special relativity within it. In the next post we will introduce the concept of fields on our group, and we will begin to connect our Casimir invariants and representations to those fields to construct the beginnings of a physcial theory.

item.title: 2: Representations
item.dc:creator: Tom Whitehead
item.description:
1 Representations

In the last post we looked at groups, algebras, and Casimir invariants. This time, we’re going to dive deep into the world of representation theory, and begin connecting the abstract mathematical structures of groups and algebras to the physical properties of particles in our universe.

A ‘representation’ of an algebra is a way of expressing the elements of the algebra as matrices, and the algebra operation as matrix multiplication. There are generally multiple different representations of a given algebra, and each representation can be thought of as a different way of realizing the symmetries of the algebra.

Let’s walk through the details of how to build representations using the example of the algebra , which is part of the full algebra we are looking at. First, we need to explore the structure of a bit more, and introduce the Cartan-Weyl basis of the algebra.

1.1 The Cartan-Weyl basis

We discussed last time that has three generators and a single Casimir invariant . The presence of a single Casimir invariant means that the representations of are classified by a single number, which we will call . This number is related to the eigenvalue of the Casimir invariant in the representation. The single Casimir invariant also means that there is a single-element subalgebra of , called the Cartan subalgebra, which is an abelian subalgebra. For the sake of definiteness we will denote it by . We can now carry out a mathematical trick, and make some more operators: and . Plugging these definitions into the commutation relations for , we find that and . This is the Cartan-Weyl basis of the algebra: it consists of the Cartan subalgebra , and the raising and lowering operators and , whose names will make sense shortly.

We are going to assume that the representation of we are looking at is finite-dimensional, which means that the matrices that represent the generators are finite in size. Let’s assume that we have vector space on which the generators of act. We can find a vector in this space which is an eigenvector of , so for some eigenvalue . We call the ‘weight’ of the vector . Now let’s act on with the raising operator , and measure the weight of this new vector using :

So has created a new vector with weight (hence the name ‘raising operator’). Note that the weight has increased by exactly 1: it is not a continuous change, but discrete. We could keep applying to generate vectors with higher and higher weight; but we are assuming that the representation is finite-dimensional, so at some point we must reach a vector such that . Let us call the eigenvalue of on this vector , so . We can now apply the lowering operator to generate vectors with lower and lower weight (again going down in steps of 1), until we reach a vector such that (the series must terminate because we assume a finite-dimensional representation). In the box below we prove that , and hence there are exactly vectors in the representation, with weights ranging from to in integer steps.

NoteProof that

We know two things about the vector : it has weight , so , and it is annihilated by the raising operator, so . Let’s try applying the lowering operator to , and then going back up with :

We can do something similar with an arbitrary vector which is the result of applying the lowering operator times to , so : let us first define the eigenvalue of on this vector as . Now let us consider the vector , and apply to it:

so This is an arithmetic progression: with the starting point we can solve it to find that . Now we know that (because ), so we can solve for to find that .

Now to connect to some physics. The number is called the ‘spin’ of the representation, and in order for to be an integer, can take on any non-negative half-integer value (i.e. ). This is exactly the spin associated with all the particles of the Standard Model.

1.2 Other algebras

The Cartan-Weyl basis and the method of raising and lowering operators can be applied to any simple Lie algebra, including . Because has two Casimir invariants, its representations are classified by two integers, which we can call and . The raising and lowering operators of come in pairs, and they raise and lower the weights in a two-dimensional weight space. The representations of are more complicated than those of , but they can still be constructed using the same principles. The representation labels are both integers, rather than half-integers, but this is just a normalization convention: the spin of is a half-integer because we have normalized the generators such that the raising and lowering operators change the weight by 1, whereas for we have normalized the generators such that the raising and lowering operators change the weights by 1 in a two-dimensional weight space. The representations of are important in particle physics, as they correspond to the different types of quarks and gluons in the Standard Model.

As an abelian algebra, has only one-dimensional representations, which are classified by a single integer (or half-integer) charge .

The representations of are more complicated. Because the algebra is non-compact, its representations are infinite-dimensional. We can split the two labels (as there are two Casimir invariants) into a continuous label (which will end up related to mass) and a discrete label (which will end up related to spin).

2 Casimir values

We have seen that the representations of the algebras can be labelled by a set of numbers (e.g.  for , and for ). Physically it can be more intuitive to label the representations by the values of the Casimir invariants, as these are directly related to measurable physical properties of particles and fields. We conclude this post by summarising the values of the Casimir invariants in the different representations.

For the value of the Casimir invariant in the representation with spin is given by , familiar from angular momentum in quantum mechanics. For the value of the Casimir invariant in the representation with charge is given by . For the value of the Casimir invariant in the representation with labels is given by and .

For the values of the Casimir invariants in the representation with labels are given by and . There are three different branches of representations of : a primary branch, where is a real number; a complementary branch where is pure imaginary; and a discrete series, where takes on specific values. In the next post we will connect these values to physical properties of particles in our constructed universe.

item.title: 1: The Playground
item.dc:creator: Tom Whitehead
item.description:
1 Introduction

Group field theory is a beautiful mathematical idea: that the whole of the universe emerges from the interplay of symmetries. We will begin with the simplest possible foundation: defining a set of symmetries, and then building a universe out of them. This is the playground, where we set up a series of simple rules, and then see what happens when we let them run.

The ‘group’ in ‘group field theory’ refers to a mathematical structure that captures the idea of symmetry. A group is a set of elements, along with an operation that combines two elements to produce a third. These elements represent symmetries, and the operation represents how these symmetries combine. For example, the symmetries of a square can be represented by a group consisting of rotations (by 90, 180, and 270 degrees), reflections in both the horizontal and vertical axes and through the corners, and the identity transformation (doing nothing). These symmetries can be combined in various ways: for example, a 90 degree rotation followed by a reflection across the vertical axis is equivalent to a reflection across the diagonal. The group captures all of these relationships between symmetries.

The symmetries of a square also include an important concept in group field theory: they are not commutative. This means that order in which you carry out the symmetry operations is important. For example, if you first rotate the square by 90 degrees and then reflect it across the vertical axis, you will end up in a different position than if you first reflect it and then rotate it. This non-commutativity is a key feature of the symmetries that we will be working with in group field theory, and it leads to rich and complex behavior when we start to combine these symmetries in various ways.

2 The group

Let us introduce the group that we will be using to build our universe. It is a relatively complicated group, so once we have introduced it we will break it down and explain what each component means. The group is

Let’s break it down. The first part, , is the group of symmetries of a four-dimensional spacetime with one time dimension and three spatial dimensions. The second part, , is the group of symmetries associated with electromagnetism. The third part, , is the group of symmetries associated with the weak nuclear force. The fourth part, , is the group of symmetries associated with the strong nuclear force. We have chosen this group based on observations of our universe: it was a specific choice that we made in order to build a universe that resembles our own. However, we could have chosen a different group, and we would have ended up with a different universe. This is the playground: we can choose any group we like, and then see what kind of universe emerges from it.

Equation 1 is a special type of group called a semisimple Lie group. This gives it some very nice mathematical properties that we will be using to create our universe. In particular, Lie groups have a corresponding Lie algebra, which is a mathematical structure that captures the infinitesimal symmetries of the group. The Lie algebra allows us to work with the symmetries in a more manageable way, and it will be a key tool in our construction of the universe. Each element of a Lie group can be expressed as the exponential of an element of its Lie algebra , which is a very powerful property to help us analyse the group. As the group Equation 1 is a direct product of four underlying groups, the algebra is a direct sum of the underlying algebras (compare to how normal exponentials work: ),

Each of these algebras is formed of a set of ‘generators’, which are the building blocks of the symmetries. The generators of are denoted by , where and run from 0 to 4. The generators combine together through a set of rules called the commutation relations, which capture how much the symmetries fail to commute. A commutator is defined simply as . The commutation relations for are given by

where . This looks a lot like the metric of special relativity, just with an extra spatial dimension: this will turn out not to be a coincidence!

only has a single generator, which we will denote by . The commutation relations for are trivial: (because there is only one generator, and a generator always commutes with itself). An algebra where all generators commute is called ‘abelian’.

has three generators, which we will denote by where runs from 1 to 3. The commutation relations for are given by

where is the Levi-Civita symbol, which is defined as and is antisymmetric in its indices (i.e. , and ). As not all commutation relations are zero the algebra is non-abelian. The commutation relations for are more complicated, but they have a similar structure to those of , built from generators where runs from 1 to 8.

3 Casimir invariants

A very important concept in group field theory is that of Casimir invariants. A Casimir invariant is a quantity that is constructed from the generators of the algebra, and that commutes with all of the generators. This means that it is a quantity that is conserved under the symmetries of the group. For example, for there are two Casimir invariants. The first is given by

and the second is formed using the Levi-Civita symbol again as

Next time we will investigate the physical meaning of these Casimir invariants. But let us finish this post with the Casimir invariants for the other algebras. For there is only one Casimir invariant, which is simply . For there is also only one Casimir invariant, which is given by . is again a bit more complicated: it has two Casimir invariants, which are given by

where the symmetric invariant tensor . The Casimir invariants are very important because they will turn out to be related to the physical properties of the particles in our universe. For example, the Casimir invariant will turn out to be related to the mass of particles, while will be related to the weak isospin of particles. We will dive into this correspondence between Casimir invariants and physical properties in the next post.
